raw_response:
## SOC 2 CC6.4 Readiness Findings — Physical Access Restriction

Based on the physical security policy and Q4 2025 badge access log, Helios Systems has documented physical access control procedures; however, several gaps were identified that may impact readiness for SOC 2 CC6.4.

| # | Finding | Related Section | Why It’s a Gap | Severity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Terminated employee retained physical access after termination | Policy §4.3 — Termination and Deactivation | The access log shows Rachel Simmons, marked as **Terminated** with a termination date of **2025-11-15**, was granted access to the main office on **2025-11-17** via badge **B-2847**. Policy requires badge deactivation within 24 hours of the termination effective date and collection of the badge on the last day of employment. This indicates either the badge was not deactivated timely or was not collected. | **High** |
| 2 | Badge deactivation control appears ineffective | Policy §4.3 — Termination and Deactivation | Beyond the individual event, the log demonstrates that the access control system allowed a terminated employee status to remain active for physical entry. This suggests the termination workflow between HR, Facilities, and the access control system may not be operating effectively or lacks automated enforcement. | **High** |
| 3 | CCTV retention requirement is inconsistent within the policy | Policy §5.3 — CCTV Retention; Appendix A — System Configuration Summary | Section 5.3 requires CCTV footage to be retained for a minimum of **90 days**, but Appendix A states the Milestone XProtect retention period is configured for **30 days**. This inconsistency creates ambiguity and may mean the actual system configuration does not meet the stated policy requirement. | **Medium** |
| 4 | Restricted area access approvals are not evidenced | Policy §4.1 — Badge Issuance; §3.2 — Access Points | The log shows multiple successful entries into restricted areas, including server rooms and network closets, by employees such as David Chen and Tom Bradley. While their departments appear relevant, no Access Request Forms or Security Manager approvals were provided to demonstrate that restricted access was formally authorized as required by policy. | **Medium** |
| 5 | Quarterly badge access review evidence not provided | Policy §4.4 — Quarterly Access Review | The policy requires quarterly review of all badge access authorizations by the Security Manager with department heads. No evidence of a Q4 2025 access review, reviewer sign-off, exceptions, or remediation was provided. The terminated-user access event further suggests the review or termination process may not be detecting inappropriate access promptly. | **Medium** |
| 6 | Badge access log does not cover the full Q4 period | Policy §3.1 — Badge Access System Logging; §4.4 — Quarterly Access Review | The badge log is described as Q4 2025 evidence, but entries only run from **2025-10-01 through 2025-12-05**. There are no records for **2025-12-06 through 2025-12-31**. This limits the ability to conclude that access was appropriately restricted and monitored for the full quarter. | **Medium** |
| 7 | Access log does not show activity for all documented access points | Policy §3.1 — Badge Access System; §3.2 — Access Points | The policy lists several access points, including **AP-FLOOR3-01**, **AP-EXEC-01**, and **AP-DOCK-01**, but no entries for these access points appear in the provided log. If the log is intended to represent activity across all access points, this creates an evidence gap. It is unclear whether these access points had no activity, were not operational, or were omitted from the report. | **Low** |
| 8 | Disaster recovery site physical access controls are not evidenced | Policy §2 — Scope | The policy applies to both the Raleigh corporate office and the Charlotte disaster recovery site. However, the access point list and badge log appear to relate only to the corporate office. No access points, badge logs, CCTV coverage, or access review evidence were provided for the disaster recovery site. | **Medium** |
| 9 | Visitor management controls are documented but not evidenced | Policy §6 — Visitor Management | The policy requires visitor preregistration, ID verification, escorting, badge issuance, check-out, and deactivation of unreturned visitor badges. No visitor logs, visitor badge records, escort attestations, or exception reports were provided. This limits the ability to assess whether visitor access is restricted in practice. | **Medium** |
| 10 | Restricted server room multi-factor requirement is not evidenced in logs | Policy §3.2 — Access Points | Server rooms **AP-SRV-01** and **AP-SRV-02** require **HID iCLASS SE + PIN**, but the provided log only shows badge swipe events and granted access results. There is no evidence that PIN authentication was required or successfully validated for server room entries. | **Medium** |

## Summary Assessment

Helios Systems has a reasonable physical security policy framework, including badge access, restricted zones, CCTV, visitor management, and termination procedures. However, readiness gaps exist in both **control design consistency** and **operating effectiveness**.

The most significant issue is the **post-termination access granted to Rachel Simmons**, which directly conflicts with the termination deactivation requirement and indicates a potentially material weakness in physical access revocation.

Before SOC 2 audit readiness, Helios Systems should remediate the terminated-user access issue, reconcile CCTV retention settings, provide evidence of restricted access approvals, complete quarterly access reviews, and ensure full-period access logs are available for all facilities and access points.
